opposition: ["The state of Wisconsin filed a motion to have a ban on same sex marriage in the state constitution struck down. support: ['MDY Industries, LLC v. Blizzard Entertainment, Inc. et al., --- F.3d ----, at 47, 2010 WL5141269 (9th Cir. 2010) The Ninth Circuit explained, MDY has never been a copyright infringer. The Court must vacate the DMCA section of its permanent injunction. MDY is once again entitled to safe harbor for its violation of the DMCA. If MDY breached the DMCA, it is not liable under the DMCA despite its (a)(2) violation. The DMCA provides safe Harbor for a defined subset of violators. Violators who are also copyrightinfringers are not eligible. The safe harbor could not protect MDY until the Ninth Circuit reversed its decision. The case is still before the U.S. District Court.']
opposition: ["MDY’s motion to vacate the permanent injunction imposed under 17 U.S.C.§ 1201(a)(2) hinges on a nonsensical contention—that enforcing the judgment on a claim they lost on appeal would be “inconsistent” with the Ninth Circuit’'s opinion. The Ninth Circuit spoke, in no uncertain terms, to the precise issue. This Court is bound to sustain the injunction against MDY under the rule of mandate doctrine. MDY was a infringer prior to the parties’ cross-appeals, and its failure to raise itsmeritless defense at that time precludes it from doing so now, the court says. The court concludes that MDY has no right to appeal the ruling."]

support: ['The University\'s denial of a forum for Professor Ayers\'s public speech is an unconstitutional prior restraint on such speech. A plaintiff in a First Amendment case must meet four conditions to obtain a preliminary injunction. The reason for the presumption is self-evident, as the Supreme Court put it, "[t]he loss of freedoms for even minimal periods constitutes irreparable injury" The University must show that the threatened injury to the Plaintiff outweighs any harm the proposed injunction may cause the opposing party. The injunction must not be contrary to the public interest. The First Amendment should not be infringed on the freedom of speech of each plaintiff, thus creating a strong presumption that a Preliminary Injunction should issue. The University should be held to the same standard as the U.S. District Court for the District of Wyoming.', 'Professor William Ayers is suing the University of Wyoming for First Amendment violations. He says he was denied the right to speak on campus on April 28, 2010. The University responded that they are opposed to the motion for a Preliminary injunction. The motion for preliminary injunction was filed on April 15, 2010, and will be heard on April 16, 2010 at 10:30 a.m. at the U.S. District Court for the District of Wyoming in Cheyenne. For confidential support call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-8255 or visit http://www.suicidepreventionlifeline.org/. opposition: ['This case arises from a request made by Plaintiff Meghan Lanker to rent the University of Wyoming Sports Complex for a speech proposed to be given by Professor William Ayers on April 28, 2010. The University, through its President, Defendant Tom Buchanan, has refused the request on public safety and security grounds. The earlier appearance, which is not in issue here, was cancelled by the faculty member who had invited Ayers. To obtain a preliminary injunction, Plaintiffs have the burden to establish the following: (1) a substantial likelihood of success on the merits; (2) they will suffer irreparable injury unless the injunction issues; (3) the balance of equities tips in their favor; and (4) an injunction is in the public interest. Where the preliminary injunction would require the nonmoving party to take a full trial, the burden on the movants would more closely scrutinize the case.']

Instead, it has designated two in-house employees, Jon Kennedy, its SeniorProject Manager, and Alison Adams, the manager of its Source Rotation & Environmental Protection Department, for this purpose. Neither Adams nor Kennedy prepared a report outlining their calculation of damages. Their opinions as a whole are unreliable, do not fit the facts of the case, and therefore. do not meet the requirements of FED. R.R. 702 for admissibility.']
opposition: ['Tampa Bay Water, a Regional Water Supply Authority, is the Plaintiff in this case. Tampa Bay Water has filed a motion to strike the Joint Affidavit of Kevin D. Dennis, CPA/CFF, CMA, and E. Allen Jacobs, Ph.D. in Support of HDR’s Motion to Exclude Testimony of Alison. Adams and Jon Kennedy on Damages Due to “Lost Water” Use and for Sanctions. The joint affidavit contains a number of errors that should not be considered in support of the Daubert Motion. TBW states that it anticipates that some testimony will still be in the form of their testimony based on their education, training, and/or education of others. The Joint Aff affidavit mischaracterizes the evidence regarding whether the cracking of the soil-cement is a safety issue and whether the short-term repair program put in place by TBW after it discovered the cracking is sufficient.']

opposition: ['Justices Breyer, Thomas, Roberts, Sotomayor, and Kennedy said that 8 U.S.C. § 1252(b)(9) did not apply in the specific context of Jennings. Justices Alito, Thomas and Kennedy agreed with the majority of the court on that point. Justice Breyer’s dissenting opinion said that the aliens challenged “their detention without bail, not an order of removal,” and that the law applies only “[w]ith respect to review of an Order of removal under [§1252(a)(1)].” Id. at *44 (Breyer, J., dissenting) (quoting § 12 52(b), alterations in original). Justices Roberts and Kennedy, along with Justices Thomas, did not limit the law to challenges to orders of removal.']

opposition: ['Epic Systems Corporation has imposed upon its employees an unjustmandate to individually arbitrate their wage claims without the benefit of their collective right to engage in concerted activity with fellow employees. This class waiver provision cannot be enforced without offending the purpose of the National Labor Relations Act and Fair Labor Standards Act. The contract is not a product of mutual agreement or meaningful choice, but of Epic’s forced “assent” from Plaintiff, who could neither seek understanding of the contract nor signal repudiation of its terms. Epic has in fact promised nothing in exchange for its employees losing their legal right to have their claims heard collectively in court. The terms of this contract to arbitrate explicitly permit Epic to escape any declarediablypromise to be bound by them. Epic also retained the unilateral and unlimited ability to change or eliminate the contract altogether.']

opposition: ['Plaintiff failed to satisfy the stringent standard for reconsideration under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 60(b) ‘reconsideration ‘is an extraordinary remedy to be employed sparingly.’ Relief under Rule 60( b) is available only when one of the following: mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect; (2) newly uncovered evidence that, with reasonable diligence, could not have been discovered in time to move for a new trial under Rule 59(b); (3) fraud (whether previously called intrinsic or extrinsic), misrepresentation, or misconduct by an opposing party; (4) the judgment is void; or (5) it is based on an earlier judgment that has been satisfied, released or discharged. “[A] Pyrrhicmotion for. reconsideration is not a vehicle ‘to reiterate arguments previously made’']

['The United States is seeking an injunction to stop the construction of a casino resort. The Quapaw Tribe of Oklahoma is the only entity involved in the project. The Court has no jurisdiction to hear claims seeking to divest the United States of title to the land it holds in trust or restricted fee for the Quapawed Tribe. Therefore, injunctive relief is unnecessary and Plaintiff cannot show the requisite likelihood of success on the merits of either of its two statutory claims. The court should reject the Motion for Preliminary Injunction because it does not offer the relief Plaintiff seeks, and because the injunction it seeks is unavailable. The motion should also be denied because the Court lacks jurisdiction to enjoin the Tribe, which is not before this Court, and therefore, the Court cannot grant it.']

opposition: ["Incarcerated Entertainment, LLC (“IE”) files its Response in Opposition to Warner Bros. Pictures’ Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff’s First Amended Complaint. support: ['Cellular South filed a motion to amend its complaint against AT&T and others. The U.S. District Court for the Western District of Mississippi denied the motion. The court ruled that the Motion to Amend should be granted under the rule of liberal pleading. The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals has time and again reinforced the liberal standard set forth in Foman v. Davis (1962). The court also ruled that an amendment should not be made for purposes of undue delay, bad faith, or dilatory motive on the part of the movant, as well as undue prejudice to the opposing party by virtue of allowance of the amendment, futility of amendment, etc.—the rules require to be “freely given.” The motion to Amend was filed on October 1, 2012.', 'Cellular South seeks leave to amend its complaint against AT&T, Motorola Solutions, Motorola Mobility, and John Does 1-10. The court should grant leave under Rule 15(a) of FED. R. CIV. P. C IV15(a), which provides that “[t]he court should freely give leave [to amend] when justice so requires” The Fifth Circuit has reiterated the liberal standard for determining whether leave should be given to amend a complaint. The decision should be decided on the merits of the case, not on the basis of any apparent reason or reason that the amendment would cause undue delay or prejudice to the opposing party. The jury should be allowed to decide whether or not to grant the motion to amend the complaint, and if so, by how much time it would take.']

opposition: ['Mo Hailong and Mo Yun are charged in a one-count indictment with conspiring to steal trade secrets. Trial for both defendants is scheduled for December 1, 2014. The Eighth Circuit has recognized that joint trials are almost always proper, and are nearly universally proper for defendants charged in the same conspiracy and face no other charges. Separate trials would force the parties and the court to conduct the same lengthy,complicated trial twice, and this result would prejudice the government. The Speedy Trial Act contains the same preference for joint trials that theseverance doctrine protects, and time should be excluded under the Act for delaynecessary to secure a joint trial in this case. A trial of the defendants would be lengthy—at least a month, if not longer. And, as explained in more detail below, the evidence at a trial of a defendant would be nearly identical to the trial of her brother.', 'The Government argues that the case against Mo Yun involves no evidence of her involvement in the charged conspiracy during any time period in which an alleged theft of trade secrets took place. Ms. Mo asks the Court to exercise its discretion and sever her trial from that of Mo Hailong. She argues that severance is necessary to accord respect to her rights to a speedy and fair trial. The Government must acknowledge the enormous gap in time between the 2007-08 excerpts and the 2011-12 alleged acts of theft – a gap which includes Ms.Mo terminating her employment with DBN no later than March 2009. The U.S. Government contends that its problems timely producing discovery in this case, together with Ms.mo’s intended motions, preclude a December 1 trial.']

support: ['Jordan Hamlett is charged with false representation of a social security number. He is also accused of trying to obtain a copy of tax returns belonging to President Donald J. Trump (who at the time was “President Elect”) Hamlett claims the statements he made to agents of the U.S. Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA) and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) were made in the absence of counsel. The Government must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that the accused made statements voluntarily. The requirements that statements be voluntary is drawn both from the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and the Due Process Clause. opposition: ["The United States opposes Jordan Hamlett’s (the “Defendant”) motion to suppress. Hamlett contends, first, that agents violated his Miranda rights, and, second, that his statements were involuntary. Both arguments should be rejected, and the motion denied in its entirety. The Fifth Circuit has explained that the only relevant inquiry is how areasonable man in the suspect's position would have understood his situation. The burden of proving that the defendant was in custody rests with the defendant, not the officer. The court should reject the motion for two reasons: first, agents weren't required to Mirandize the Defendant, and second, the defendant's statements were voluntary. The defendant's motion should also be denied for the reason that he wasn't in custody at the time of the interview.", 'The United States opposes Jordan Hamlett’s (hereinafter, the “Defendant”) motion to suppress. The Defendant contends, first, that agents violated his Miranda rights, and, second, that his statements were involuntary. There was no Miranda violation because the Defendant was not in custody at the time of his interview. Miranda warnings must be given prior to interrogation only when a suspect is in custody, and no warnings need be given when he is not. “The reasonable person through whom [the court] view[s] the situation must be neutral to the environment and to the purposes of the investigation,” the court said in a previous ruling. The United States reserves its right to respond accordingly to the Defendant’S motion.']

support: ['The Plaintiffs are sheepherders admitted into the United States under an H-2A nonimmigrant visa that allows a foreign national to work in temporary or seasonal agricultural work. The terms and conditions of such temporary work are governed by regulations adopted by the U.S. Department of Labor. The appropriate method of resolving a claim of breach of contract is before the Department ofLabor and its administrative procedures, not the courts. The Federal District Court is a court of limited jurisdiction possessing only the power to act under the Constitution and Congress. The courts must “presume that a cause of action lies outside this limited jurisdiction, and establish the burden of establishing the contrary upon the party asserting the claim.” The courts have no power to consider claims for which they lack subject matter jurisdiction.']
opposition: ['The H-2A program is built around "work contracts", as defined in 20 C.F.R.103 (b): "All the material terms and conditions of employment relating to wages, hours, working conditions, and other benefits, including those required by 8 U.S.C. 188, 29 CFR part 501" Plaintiffs have a right to enforce their contracts with defendant Fernandez in a court of law quite separate and apart from any investigative actions taken by the United States Department of Labor. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction over plaintiffs\' federal claims raised under the Fair Labor Standards Act. Despite defendants\' allegations of "back door" claims, plaintiffs\' state contract and wage claims are enforceable contracts between the employer and the H- 2A worker.']

opposition: ['KAIL MARIE, MICHELLE L. BROWN, and KERRY WILKS, Ph.D., and DONNA.DITRANI, Plaintiffs, are asking for a temporary restraining order or preliminary injunction against Douglas and Sedgwick County District Court Clerks Douglas Hamilton and Bernie Lumbreras. The Kansas Supreme Court has already placed a stay on issuing marriage licenses to same-sex couples in Johnson County, Kansas, citing “the interest of establishing statewide consistency.” Plaintiffs’ request would conflict with the Kansas SupremeCourt’s Order, but also its supervisory authority over all of the clerks in Kansas. It would also lead to a confusing and non-uniform situation in which clerks in two counties are doing one thing and the other 103 are doing something else, implicating important federalist concerns by disrupting the judicial system.', "The court should deny plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order or Preliminary Injunction. The relief requested against Defendants Hamilton and Lumbreras is beyond the Court’s subject matter jurisdiction because it is sought against statejudicial officers. No case anywhere in the country, state or federal, has held, implied, or suggested that the use of the terms ‘bride’ and ‘groom’ on marriage licenses violates constitutional rights. Because Kansas law considers same-sex marriages to be legally void andunenforceable, only a final judgment binding on all Kansas officials can provide plaintiffs with anything more than the appearance of marriage. There is not even a remote possibility of success on the merits on plaintiffs' claim for relief against Kansas Department of Health and Environment Secretary Moser."]

support: ['Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington, or “Plaintiff,’ filed a lawsuit under the Federal Records Act (“FRA”) to challenge as arbitrary and capricious a Department of Education “policy’ that does not exist. The Department, however, has never had a policy authorizing its employees to. conduct government business on personal email accounts. This action, which purports tochallenge that policy, is therefore completely unfounded. Even if the challenged policy did exist – and it emphatically does not – Plaintiff lacks standing to bring this action under the FRA. The case is filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Florida, in Tallahassee, Florida. The defendants are Morris Young, the Sheriff in and for Gadsdale County, Florida, Eric. McMillon, individually, and JoZell. McWhite, individually. The plaintiffs are the Estate of James. MICHAEL KINSAUL and his wife, Zachariah.']
opposition: ['The plaintiffs, ZACHARIAH KINSAUL and ALETA KIN SAUL, file their response in opposition to Defendants’ Motion for Summary Judgment. To win, Defendants must show that “everything in the record . . . demonstrates that no genuine issue of material fact exists.” Genuine issues of fact are those where the evidence is such that a reasonable jury could return a verdict for the non-movant. “Credibility determinations”, such as believability of witnesses, “can be made only after trial, and not on a motion for summary judgment,” the Eleventh Circuit has said. The court must accept the truth of Plaintiff’s allegations and evidence and “must draw all reasonable inferences in Plaintiff”s favor.']

support: ['The case was brought by Target Training International against Extended Creditor of North America. The court ruled that the case should be stayed pending a reexamination of the case. support: ['Roca Labs, Inc (“ROCA LABS”), by the undersigned counsel, hereby moves the U.S. District Court to Remand this case to the Circuit Court of Hillsborough County, Florida from which it was removed. The grounds supporting this motion are set forth in the Memorandum of Law that follows below. The original Complaint pled separate claims for Defamation Per Se against the two Defendants seeking the recovery of damages incurred by Plaintiff as a result of actions Defendantsperpetrated. In the Complaint, Roca LABS did not demand a specific amount of damages. Instead, Plaintiff simply alleged that the state court’s jurisdictional minimum of $15,000.00 had been met. That fact alone renders Defendants’Notice of Removal defective, and this Court should summarily dismiss the Notice of Removal.']
opposition: ["Roca Labs manufactures food additives which markets to the public as a weight loss option “as a surgery-free alternative to gastric bypass.” Defendants published an article on the Plaintiff’s blog concerning Plaintiff entitled “Roca labs Weigh Loss Scam” on September 22, 2014. Plaintiff alleges that the article accused it of various fraudulent activities. As a result of the alleged defamation by Defendants, Plaintiff alleges it has suffered damages, which “include, but are not limited to, the failure of Roca to sell its product to identifiable potential customers, as well as harm to its reputation . . .” Roca is seeking injunctive relief, damages against both defendants in “an amount fair and just to account for its money damages, interest, reasonable attorney's fees and costs incurred”"]

support: ['Chase is seeking to strike Christopher Wyatt’s expert report regarding its alleged failure to convert Trial Period Plans (“TPPs”) into permanent loan modifications as purportedly required under the Home AffordableModification Program (‘HAMP’) Chase moves this Court to issue an order striking the expert report in its entirety. Federal Rule of Evidence 702 makes clear, expert testimony is admissible only if it is both relevant and reliable. The expert report is not relevant because it merely states legal conclusions, and it fails toestablish a reliable basis for his opinions. The court should strike the report because it consists of nothing more than ‘inadmissible’ and ‘unreliable’ testimony, the court says. The report should be struck because it does not state an issue of law, which is not a proper subject of testimony.']
opposition: ['Christopher Wyatt is an expert in the mortgage loan servicing industry. He has worked for multiple lenders and as a private consultant on mortgage servicing issues. His opinions cover only the following issues: Whether Chase routinely fails to comply with HAMP and why. The likely number of class members in Massachusetts. Chase’s failure to convert TPPs into permanent modifications. The consequences to homeowners of failure of the class to convert the TPPs. Mr. Wyatt concludes that homeowners who receive. delayed/unconverted modifications will suffer increased interest and delinquency charges, increased. credit reporting charges, loss of valuable credit reporting benefits, and. stressful debt collection activities even if no foreclosure. occurs. Chase v. Shine, Decl. Decl. of Karen Shine (Shine, Decl., Decl. and Decl. “Legal Conclusions, Not Conclusions”).']

The request has been sent to the City Attorney's Office in Charlottesville, Virginia and is expected to be heard on August 11, 2017 at 10:30 a.m."]
opposition: ['Jason Kessler is suing the City of Charlottesville for permission to hold a rally in Emancipation Park. The City has said that the park is unable to accommodate a large crowd of this size. Kessler has never bothered to update or revise the outdated estimate of 400 people that he submitted for the rally. The Defendants submit the following Brief in opposition to Plaintiff Kessler’s Motion for a Preliminary Injunction or Temporary Restraining Order filed by the Plaintiff. In the Complaint, the Plaintiff seems to attach great importance to the fact that other events have taken place in Charlottesville without being moved to a different location, as well as a privately-run music festival. The Brief also includes a statement from the Defendants that they do not believe that injunctive relief from the court is necessary.']
